channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Stormgate Online Modes Disappearing Soon, Offline Play Coming
item.content:encoded:
Developer and publisher Frost Giant Studios has revealed that its RTS Stormgate will be taken offline soon thanks to a corporate buyout, but an offline play mode is on the way, so you should still be able to play the game in some form.

As spotted by Delisted Games, news of Stormgate being taken offline was posted on the game's official Discord server, with Frost Giant revealing that its multiplayer partner Hathora "has been purchased by an AI company". I bet Frost Giant regrets using generative AI during the game's development now.

Hathora will wind down its multiplayer providing services at the end of April, which will leave Stormgate's multiplayer modes without a host. Frost Giant says it's working on an offline play patch for the game, but online modes "will not be available" when Hathora shuts down.

Frost Giant goes on to say that it hopes to restore online play at some point in the future, but that doing so will be contingent on "finding a partner to support ongoing operations", so it doesn't sound like the developer is confident that will happen.

Prior to offline mode arriving, Frost Giant is promising more information about how it will work and whether any patches will be released before the server shutdown.

If they're not, then it means there will be a period of time, whether that's days, weeks, or even months, during which Stormgate won't be playable at all. Right now, you need an internet connection even to play the game's single-player campaign.

Stormgate was first revealed all the way back in 2022. It's a free-to-play RTS from some of the folks who worked at Blizzard, and its Kickstarter campaign managed to amass more than $2.4 million at its close, having smashed through its initial goal in just 15 minutes.

Stormgate is available to play for free right now on PC via Steam, although it won't be for much longer thanks to Hathora's server shutdown.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/stormgate-online-modes-disappearing-soon
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: MindsEye Sales "Doubling Almost Weekly", CEO Claims
item.content:encoded:
MindsEye may seem like a flop to you (and, indeed, to most right-thinking people), but according to Build a Rocket Boy CEO Mark Gerhard, the game's sales are increasing, and he's still alleging some of the game's negative reception was "orchestrated".

Gerhard makes these claims in a new interview with Games Beat's Dean Takahashi, wherein he also admits that MindsEye suffered "the worst launch in history". That honestly feels like a bit of an understatement to me.

According to Gerhard, however, MindsEye's fortunes are turning around; the game is now "being very well-reviewed", he says. I'm not entirely sure where he means to suggest that's happening, though; over on Steam, the game still has a Mixed rating, even across 91 recent reviews.

Gerhard also says that MindsEye sales are "doubling almost weekly", which feels like a strange claim to make. Steam numbers aren't everything, of course, but at time of writing, seven people are playing MindsEye, with the game's 24-hour player peak reaching just 16 on Steam.

Of course, it's possible that sales of the game had completely dried up, and that Gerhard's claim is still technically correct. After all, if one person buys a game one week, then two people buy it the next week, that does mean sales are doubling.

By saying that, though, I'm probably making myself part of what Gerhard still alleges was a deliberately-constructed sabotage campaign engineered at launch and aimed at ensuring MindsEye failed. Gerhard has also previously blamed layoffs on so-called "espionage".

Gerhard tells Takahashi that Build a Rocket Boy has "very strong evidence" of the "orchestrated" negativity campaign, and that both UK and US authorities are "assisting [Build a Rocket Boy] with [its] investigation".

Despite Build a Rocket Boy's split with former publisher IO Interactive, the developer apparently intends to continue working with external partners in future, although Gerhard says that self-publishing will allow the studio to "provide phenomenal value" by pricing MindsEye lower.

It is, of course, possible that Gerhard and Build a Rocket Boy can turn MindsEye around, and it's also possible that the aforementioned "orchestrated" campaign did take place, but whether or not players decide to give the game another chance is a different matter entirely.

MindsEye is available right now on PC, PlayStation 5, and Xbox Series X|S. You can read our review of the game right here.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/mindseye-ceo-mark-gerhard-comments-sales
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Heroes of Might and Magic: Olden Era Release Date Revealed
item.content:encoded:
Publisher Hooded Horse and developer Unfrozen have revealed the release date for upcoming fantasy turn-based strategy game Heroes of Might and Magic: Olden Era, and the game is coming sooner than you might think.

As announced in a brand new trailer (as well as in a Steam blog post), Heroes of Might and Magic: Olden Era will be arriving in Early Access on April 30th. It's the first mainline installment in the series in more than ten years.

Heroes of Might and Magic: Olden Era's Early Access release will include quite a lot of content at the outset; you can look forward to six factions, as well as a full tutorial and the first act of the game's campaign, along with several game modes and even online multiplayer.

Naturally, since this is an Early Access release, more will be added over the course of the game's development. Unfrozen and Hooded Horse say they're planning to stay in Early Access "for a year, but that could change" depending on what needs to be done to address player feedback.

First announced all the way back in 2024, Heroes of Might and Magic: Olden Era is the next mainline installment in the Heroes of Might and Magic series, which began with 1995's eponymous title.

Prior to Olden Era, the last major release in the franchise was 2015's Heroes of Might and Magic VII, although Unfrozen and Hooded Horse have opted to refrain from using a number to title this one.

You can check out the release date trailer for Heroes of Might and Magic: Olden Era, which shows off how the two studios have chosen to continue the franchise's legacy, right here.

Heroes of Might and Magic: Olden Era launches for PC via Steam Early Access on April 30th. Hopefully, it won't be too long before we know when the full version is arriving.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/heroes-of-might-and-magic-olden-era-release-date
